message.abstract:
<jats:title>Abstract</jats:title>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Background</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>We perform a phenome-wide scan to identify pre-existing conditions related to COVID-19 susceptibility and prognosis across the medical phenome and how they vary by race.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Methods</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>The study is comprised of 53,853 patients who were tested/positive for COVID-19 between March 10 and September 2, 2020 at a large academic medical center.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Results</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    Pre-existing conditions strongly associated with
                    <jats:underline>hospitalization</jats:underline>
                    were
                    <jats:italic>renal failure, pulmonary heart disease</jats:italic>
                    , and
                    <jats:italic>respiratory failure</jats:italic>
                    . Hematopoietic conditions were associated with
                    <jats:underline>ICU admission/mortality</jats:underline>
                    and mental disorders were associated with
                    <jats:underline>mortality</jats:underline>
                    in non-Hispanic Whites. Circulatory system and genitourinary conditions were associated with
                    <jats:underline>ICU admission/mortality</jats:underline>
                    in non-Hispanic Blacks.
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Conclusions</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Understanding pre-existing clinical diagnoses related to COVID-19 outcomes informs the need for targeted screening to support specific vulnerable populations to improve disease prevention and healthcare delivery.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
title: A phenome-wide association study (PheWAS) of COVID-19 outcomes by race using the electronic health records data in Michigan Medicine
